items.title: What's climate change doing to avalanches and how we predict them?
Why more Fort McMurray non-profits are looking to side hustles to survive
items.summary_auto: Fort McMurray's fundraising scene was once dominated by generous corporate donors and juicy government grants. Now, a tough economy is making some community non-profits adopt a business mindset to remain open.

items.title: Russia sentences 19 people for deadly concert hall attack that killed 149 people
items.summary_auto: A court in Moscow on Thursday convicted 19 people of involvement in the 2024 shooting rampage at a Moscow concert hall that killed 149 people and wounded over 600 in one of the deadliest attacks in the capital in years.

items.title: Oil spill in St. Clair River 'contained,' according to Suncor in Sarnia, Ont.
items.summary_auto: The refinery says it 'immediately deployed' equipment including booms and vacuum equipment.

items.title: Oil spill in St. Clair River 'contained,' according to Suncor in Sarnia
items.summary_auto: The refinery says it 'immediately deployed' equipment including booms and vacuum equipment.

items.title: Enmax open to mayor's call for it to run Calgary's water system
items.summary_auto: The utility wasn't as warm to Jeromy Farkas's idea that it sell a U.S. power company to help cut Calgary rates.

items.title: Manitoba asks Sobeys to scrap property restrictions near its stores or it'll go to municipal board
items.summary_auto: Manitoba is down to one grocery giant still using property restrictions to keep potential competitors away from its stores after the province curbed the practice through legislation it passed last year.

items.title: How shipping companies are navigating danger in the Strait of Hormuz
'We're still a mess': N.S. man who lost 3 loved ones in crash speaks out over driver's sentence
items.summary_auto: A Nova Scotia man who lost three loved ones in a fatal 2023 crash says the 4½-year sentence the person who caused the crash received is not harsh enough. "We all got hard lives," says Adam Gabriel. "That gives him no right to do what he did."

items.title: Musicians launch reggae talent search for emerging Canadian artists
items.summary_auto: A new talent search launched by London Reggae Fest organizers aims to support emerging reggae artists across Canada.

items.title: 'It guts us’: Curator fears for future of Joggins Fossil Institute after provincial funding cuts
items.summary_auto: The curator of the Joggins Fossil Institute says a 20 per cent cut to its provincial funding will devastate operations and force the cancellation of educational programs that reach hundreds of students each year.

items.title: In Miami, Cuban exiles hope to rebuild country as they await Trump's move on regime change
items.summary_auto: While the war marches on in Iran, exiled Cubans in South Florida are keenly waiting, watching and wondering when the U.S. administration will turn its full attention south.

items.title: The Oscars are getting an award for best casting. About time, casting directors say
items.summary_auto: For the first time since 2001, the Academy Awards will be giving away a trophy for a brand new category. The professionals responsible for assembling a well-fitting cast say they're glad to have an award that puts their craft in the spotlight.

items.title: How are Hamilton children getting bear spray? Amid more crimes, police are taking action
items.summary_auto: Over the last several years, the number of Hamilton youth using bear spray in assaults and robberies has been rising, police say.

items.title: Hamilton police plan bear spray safety sessions as crimes involving kids rise
items.summary_auto: Over the last several years, the number of Hamilton youth using bear spray in assaults and robberies has been rising, police say.

items.title: How remote First Nations are working with Ornge to improve medical transportation
items.summary_auto: A new program in remote First Nations across northwestern Ontario aims to improve access to medical transportation and emergency medical service training. Communities are leading the emergency first response team (EFRT) program in partnership with the air ambulance service Ornge. Here's how the EFRT aims to improve health-care access in fly-in First Nations.

items.title: Putin is trying to pitch himself as peacemaker in Iran, but Russia also sees opportunity
items.summary_auto: The Kremlin wants to exert its influence in the Middle East and has publicly said its support for Iran, a strategic ally, is unwavering. But experts say there are limits to how far Moscow will go: It does not want to antagonize Washington, and it also stands to gain economically from a global energy crisis.

items.title: #TheMoment Canadian Natalie Wilkie won a 'full set' of Paralympic medals
items.summary_auto: Canadian Paralympic skier Natalie Wilkie tells The National about the moment she won gold, silver and bronze medals in Italy.

items.title: King Charles expressed concern over Alberta separatism in meeting with Indigenous leaders: grand chief
items.summary_auto: The grand chief of the Confederacy of Treaty 6 First Nations says King Charles "expressed his concern" after hearing about a separatist push in Alberta during a face-to-face meeting with Indigenous leaders on Wednesday.

items.title: Trump administration launches process to replace tariffs struck down by U.S. Supreme Court
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump's administration on Wednesday launched a trade investigation into excess industrial capacity in 16 major trading partners in a move to rebuild tariff pressure after the U.S. Supreme Court tore down the centerpiece of Trump's trade policy last month.

items.title: Some attendees of Taylor Swift’s Vancouver Eras Tour stop may be eligible for StubHub refunds
items.summary_auto: Attendees who bought tickets to the Vancouver dates of Taylor Swift’s Eras Tour in 2024 through StubHub could be eligible for refunds, due to an agreement reached between Consumer Protection B.C. and StubHub Canada after the company’s lack of transparency in ticket sales were found to be in violation of B.C. laws.

items.title: Social media video shows drone strike on Oman oil storage facility
items.summary_auto: A video posted to social media Wednesday shows a drone strike on an oil storage facility at Oman's Port of Salalah. CBC News verified the footage by matching the facility shown to maps and other pictures of the port, by comparing the explosion to other videos showing the facility burning and by confirming the uniform worn by a worker in the video is from a company that owns one of the ships present in the port on Wednesday.

items.title: Canada advances to World Baseball Classic playoff round for the first time
items.summary_auto: Canada has advanced to the World Baseball Classic's playoff round for the first time in the international tournament's 20-year history. The Canadians will face the U.S. in Friday's matchup.

items.title: U.S. must admit responsibility for deadly airstrike on Iranian school if at fault, says Republican
items.summary_auto: A Republican senator says the U.S. must not pretend Iran school strike didn't happen, and admit to the mistake if in fact the U.S. military was responsible for the attack.

items.title: How do you film a black hole in action?
items.summary_auto: Astronomers are capturing the first-ever video of a black hole using radio telescopes set up all over the world. Bob McDonald, host of Quirks and Quarks, explains what we could learn about the supermassive black hole at the heart of Messier 87 galaxy that's about 50 million light-years from Earth.

items.title: Old shipwreck uncovered on Sable Island
items.summary_auto: Archaeologists working on Sable Island are celebrating a rare discovery. During an excavation last May, they found surprisingly intact remains from a ship that researchers believe may have wrecked in 1812. The CBC's Andrew Sampson has the story.

items.title: Gas prices in Canada soar as U.S.-Israel war with Iran spreads
items.summary_auto: We know one of the impacts of the U.S.-Israel war in Iran is spiking the price of oil — but why did filling up our gas tanks get so costly, so fast?

items.title: Wave of overdoses in London, Ont., after police say driver may be giving out toxic drugs for free
items.summary_auto: London police are looking for a person driving around the city's downtown who was allegedly handing out drugs to people who subsequently overdosed after consuming them.

items.title: Wave of overdoses in London, Ont. after police say driver may be giving out toxic drugs for free
items.summary_auto: London police are looking for a person driving around the city's downtown who was allegedly handing out drugs to people who subsequently overdosed after consuming them.

items.title: Air Transat charging more for flights to Europe as jet fuel prices weigh on airlines
items.summary_auto: Air Transat is charging more for flights to Europe as the soaring cost of jet fuel — triggered by an oil crisis that has emerged from the war in the Middle East — weighs on Canadian and international airlines.

items.title: Do new restrictions on TikTok do enough to protect Canadians' data?
items.summary_auto: TikTok can keep existing in Canada, with new measures to protect Canadians’ data. But will the measures be effective?

items.title: Federal prisons to lose library technicians, employment co-ordinators in budget cuts
items.summary_auto: Correctional Service Canada plans to cut library technicians and employment co-ordinator positions in federal penitentiaries, a move critics worry could affect inmate recidivism and reintegration.

items.title: Why would a desalination plant be a military target? | About That
items.summary_auto: Andrew Chang explains how the war in the Middle East is threatening critical freshwater infrastructure in the region. Images provided by The Canadian Press, Reuters and Getty Images

items.title: Cuts to prison libraries at federal institutions violates human rights: advocates
items.summary_auto: The Correctional Service of Canada is finalizing plans to cut all prison librarian positions at federal institutions, in a move that advocates say violates human rights and will actively harm efforts to rehabilitate prisoners.

items.title: At least 17 killed after drone strikes school in Sudan
items.summary_auto: An explosive-laden drone blamed on Sudanese paramilitaries struck a secondary school and a health-care centre in southern Sudan Wednesday, killing at least 17 people, mostly schoolgirls, a hospital official and a medical group said.
